coredata.dc:title: Chapter 13 White Light-Emitting Novel Nanophosphors for LED Applications
coredata.prism:copyright: Copyright © 2018 Elsevier Inc. All rights reserved.
coredata.dc:creator: Nair, Govind B.
Pawade, Vijay B.
Dhoble, Sanjay J.
coredata.dc:description: White light-emitting diodes (wLEDs) have touched new heights in the lighting industry with their exceptional energy efficiency, high luminous output, marathon working life, and eco-friendly applications. Three major strategies are generally implemented to fabricate wLEDs; these include blending tricolor phosphors excited by UV light-emitting diodes (UV-LEDs) or combing a yellow phosphor with blue light-emitting diode (LED) or combing a UV excited single-phase white-emitting phosphor with UV-LED. Each of these strategies has the virtues and shortcomings of their own. In this chapter, these strategies have been described briefly and the novel phosphors for wLEDs have been enlisted. The luminescence properties of nanophosphors based on different types of host have been reviewed here. The significance of nanophosphors for LED-based applications has also been highlighted.
coredata.dcterms:subject: LED
Nanomaterials
Phosphor
White light

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Color-tunable X3MgSi2O8:Eu2+, Mn2+ (X=Ba, Sr, Ca) Nanophosphors

ce:figure.ce:label: Figure 13.1

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Transmission electron micrograph of synthesized NaAlSiO4:Dy3+ nanophosphor: (A) selected-area electron diffraction pattern pattern, (B) image showing dimension of NaAlSiO4:Dy3+ phosphor, and (C) fringe pattern of synthesized phosphor.

ce:figure.ce:source: Reproduced with permission from A. Kumar, S.J. Dhoble, D.R. Peshwe, J. Bhatt, Structural and photoluminescence properties of nepheline-structure NaAlSiO4:Dy3+ nanophosphors, J. Alloy. Comp. 609 (2014) 100–106, copyright 2014, Elsevier.

ce:figure.ce:label: Figure 13.2

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Dual emission profile of Ba5Zn4Y7.92Eu0.08O21 nanophosphor.

ce:figure.ce:source: Reproduced with permission from M. Dalal, V.B. Taxak, J. Dalal, A. Khatkar, S. Chahar, R. Devi, S.P. Khatkar, Crystal structure and Judd-Ofelt properties of a novel color tunable blue-white-red Ba5Zn4Y8O21:Eu3+ nanophosphors for near-ultraviolet based WLEDs, J. Alloy. Comp. 698 (2017) 662–672, copyright 2016, Elsevier.

ce:figure.ce:label: Figure 13.3

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic representation of energy transfer from ZnO to Pr3+.

ce:figure.ce:source: Reprinted with permission from G.H. Mhlongo, O.M. Ntwaeaborwa, H.C. Swart, R.E. Kroon, P. Solarz, W. Ryba-Romanowski, K.T. Hillie, Luminescence dependence of Pr3+ activated SiO2 nanophosphor on Pr3+ concentration, temperature, and ZnO incorporation, J. Phys. Chem. C 115 (2011) 17625–17632, copyright 2011, American Chemical Society.

ce:figure.ce:label: Figure 13.4

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) High-magnification scanning electron microscope (SEM) image, (B) low-magnification SEM image, (C) transmission electron microscopy image, and (D) energy-dispersive spectroscopy spectrum of ZrW2O8:Eu3+ nanophosphor.

ce:figure.ce:source: Reprinted with permission from J. Liao, S. Liu, H.-R. Wen, L. Nie, L. Zhong, Luminescence properties of ZrW2O8:Eu3+ nanophosphors for white light emitting diodes, Mater. Res. Bull. 70 (2015) 7–12, copyright 2015, Elsevier.

ce:figure.ce:label: Figure 13.5

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) Photoluminescence excitation spectra of ZrW2O8:Eu3+ nanophosphor. (B) Photoluminescence emission spectra of ZrW2O8:Eu3+ nanophosphors monitored at the 466-nm excitation wavelength.

ce:figure.ce:source: Reprinted with permission from J. Liao, S. Liu, H.-R. Wen, L. Nie, L. Zhong, Luminescence properties of ZrW2O8:Eu3+ nanophosphors for white light emitting diodes, Mater. Res. Bull. 70 (2015) 7–12, copyright 2015, Elsevier.

ce:figure.ce:label: Figure 13.6

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) Photoluminescence (PL) emission spectra at different temperatures excited at 460nm, and (B) the plot of PL intensity versus temperature for the LuAG:Ce and YAG:Ce nanogarnets.

ce:figure.ce:source: Reprinted with permission from R. Praveena, L. Shi, K.H. Jang, V. Venkatramu, C.K. Jayasankar, H.J. Seo, Sol–gel synthesis and thermal stability of luminescence of Lu3Al5O12:Ce3+ nano-garnet, J. Alloy. Comp. 509 (2011) 859–863, copyright 2010, Elsevier.

ce:figure.ce:label: Figure 13.7

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Photoluminescence (PL) emission spectra of (A) Ba3MgSi2O8:Eu2+, Mn2+, (B) Sr3MgSi2O8:Eu2+, Mn2+, and (C) Ca3MgSi2O8:Eu2+, Mn2+ nanophosphors.

ce:figure.ce:source: Reprinted with permission from J.S. Kim, Y.H. Park, J.C. Choi, H.L. Park, G. Chul Kim, J. Hak Yoo, Color tunability of nanophosphors by changing cations for solid-state lighting, Solid State Commun. 137 (2006) 187–190, copyright 2005, Elsevier.

ce:figure.ce:label: Figure 13.8

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Photoluminescence emission spectra of Ba2MgSi2O7:Eu2+ nanophosphors excited at 355nm.

ce:figure.ce:source: Reprinted with permission from J.-M. Kim, S.-J. Park, K.-H. Kim, H.-W. Choi, The luminescence properties of M2MgSi2O7:Eu2+ (M=Sr, Ba) nano phosphor in ultraviolet light-emitting diodes, Ceram. Int. 38 (2012) S571–S575, copyright 2011, Elsevier.

ce:figure.ce:label: Figure 13.9

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Field-emission scanning electron microscopy images of Ba3Si5O13:Eu2+ nanophosphors prepared by maintaining the Ba:Si ratio as: (A and B) 2:3, (C and D) 3:3, (E and F) 6:3, (G and H) 6:6, and (I and J) 6:10.

ce:figure.ce:source: Reprinted with permission from Z. Lu, L. Weng, S. Song, P. Zhang, X. Luo, X. Ren, Controlled synthesis of Eu2+-doped barium silicate nanostructures and their optical properties, Ceram. Int. 38 (2012) 5305–5310, copyright 2012, Elsevier.

ce:figure.ce:label: Figure 13.10

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Photoluminescence emission (A) and excitation (B) spectra of Ba3Si5O13:Eu2+ nanophosphors prepared by maintaining the Ba:Si ratio as: (black) 2:3, (red) 3:3, (green) 6:3, (blue) 6:6, and (cyan) 6:10.

ce:figure.ce:source: Reprinted with permission from Z. Lu, L. Weng, S. Song, P. Zhang, X. Luo, X. Ren, Controlled synthesis of Eu2+-doped barium silicate nanostructures and their optical properties, Ceram. Int. 38 (2012) 5305–5310, copyright 2012, Elsevier.

ce:figure.ce:label: Figure 13.11

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Photoluminescence emission spectra of Tb2(MoO4)3 nanophosphor.

ce:figure.ce:source: Reprinted with permission from K.P. Mani, G. Vimal, P.R. Biju, C. Joseph, N.V. Unnikrishnan, M.A. Ittyachen, Structural and spectral investigation of terbium molybdate nanophosphors, Spectrochim. Acta Part A Mol. Biomol. Spectrosc. 148 (2015) 412–419, copyright 2015, Elsevier.

chapter.ce:label: Chapter 13
chapter.ce:title: White Light-Emitting Novel Nanophosphors for LED Applications

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Rahstrasant Tukadoji Maharaj Nagpur University, Nagpur, India
Laxminarayan Institute of Technology, Rashtrasant Tukadoji Maharaj Nagpur University, Nagpur, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: White light-emitting diodes (wLEDs) have touched new heights in the lighting industry with their exceptional energy efficiency, high luminous output, marathon working life, and eco-friendly applications. Three major strategies are generally implemented to fabricate wLEDs; these include blending tricolor phosphors excited by UV light-emitting diodes (UV-LEDs) or combing a yellow phosphor with blue light-emitting diode (LED) or combing a UV excited single-phase white-emitting phosphor with UV-LED. Each of these strategies has the virtues and shortcomings of their own. In this chapter, these strategies have been described briefly and the novel phosphors for wLEDs have been enlisted. The luminescence properties of nanophosphors based on different types of host have been reviewed here. The significance of nanophosphors for LED-based applications has also been highlighted.

ce:section.ce:para: Recently, nanoscale materials have been receiving great attention due to their potential applications in many technologies [1–4]. Among the different fields, the nanostructured phosphor materials plays an important role in the development of display devices such as solid-state diode laser, white light-emitting diodes (wLEDs), and other advance devices used in planer display [5–7]. During device fabrication the weightage is given to the characteristics of phosphor such as high contrast, conductivity, packing density, and the size of the phosphor materials [8–10]. In the past few years, many efforts have been made to develop low-cost, highly efficient phosphor for display devices, but very few phosphors have been commercialized for the said applications. Basically, the fabrication of wLEDs requires highly efficient and good thermally stable phosphors such as blue, green, and red phosphor, which have been excited under the near UV excitation wavelength. wLEDs are a promising and energy-saving source for solid-state lighting to substitute the traditional incandescent and fluorescent lamps because of their eco-friendly characteristics and have high efficiency, long lifetime, and low energy consumption [11,12]. White light can be generated by using a combination of blue, green, and red (RGB) phosphors coupled with near-UV InGaN (Indium Gallium Nitride)-based LED chip [13,14]. However, the device performance of such a device is based on the luminescent properties of the phosphors. There are few phosphors available that are used for the wLEDs as a red, blue, and green component, and these are Y2O2S:Eu3+, ZnS:Cu+,Al3+, and BaMgAl10O17:Eu2+. Therefore, to improve the quality of light and device performance of wLEDs, there is an urgent need to develop efficient and stable RGB phosphors with good color rendering index (CRI), better crystallinity, and having strong absorption in the near-UV [15]. Besides the different phosphor hosts used in display application, the Ln3+ (Eu3+, Nd3+, Er3+)-doped YVO4 is an ideal phosphor due its high absorption and strong emissions in different spectral ranges [16–19]. Many research articles are available on the synthesis and luminescent properties of rare earth–doped YVO4 nanopowders [20–25] due to their potential applications in display and high-pressure mercury devices [26], optical telecommunications [27], and solid state lasers [28]. Thus according to the reported literature [29], it can be seen that there is an urgent demand of novel phosphor materials having spherical nanosized morphology to obtain high brightness and a high resolution. However, some authors have reported the effect of nanosized phosphor particles on luminescence quantum efficiency and energy transfer processes in lanthanide ions, when it is doped with host materials [30,31]. More recently, a researchers have reported the fundamental and unique characteristics of carbon dots (CDs) in wLEDs [32–36]. Liu et al. achieved the maximum quantum efficiency (QE) of 0.083% in 2011, in wLEDs using CDs [32]. Whereas Rogach et al. reported better color-tunable properties and good color coordinates in flexible CDs [36]. Also there are different metal nanoparticles such as Au and Ag that have received considerable attention due to their advantages in enhancing the luminescence properties of phosphor materials. In the following section of this chapter, we have discussed the different synthesis techniques used for the preparation of nanophosphor and also given an overview on some reported work on optical study of rare earth–doped inorganic phosphor for wLED applications.

In most of the cases, nanophosphors are known to produce enhanced luminescence compared with their bulk counterparts when being considered for solid-state lighting applications. Nanosized phosphor particles also enable efficient absorption of pumped blue/near-UV light. Due to their high surface-to-volume ratio and tiny particle size, they tend to produce lesser scattering of light. They also produce higher packing density when coated on a semiconductor chip [37]. In addition, nanophosphors can be coated on the LED surface in the form of thin films and requiring lesser amount of nanophosphors for coating, thereby making them cost-effective. On the other hand, there is a lot of material wastage for bulk phosphors, as they are coated in thick layers, and this further leads to lot of scattering loss. The popularity of nanophosphors has risen immensely by virtue of its superior luminescence properties to many of its bulk counterparts. Tailoring the morphology of the nanophosphors also shows a marked effect on the luminescence properties. Researchers have undertaken several strategies to develop nanosized phosphor materials with properties suitable for solid-state lighting applications [37–40]. Nanophosphors for solid-state lighting can be effectively synthesized by the solvothermal method, sol–gel method, combustion method, microwave-assisted synthesis, coprecipitation method, etc., and these methods do not require any rigorous heating at high temperatures and are cost-effective. For enhancing the optical and catalytic properties, nanoparticles are stabilized promoting core–shell formation at the nanoscale. Generally, an organic or an inorganic shell coating is preferred to promote core–shell formation of nanoparticles. In the following section, we have discussed the advantages of nanophosphors in the development of LEDs and display devices.

Today, wLEDs are an energy efficient source, which have great potential to save energy. These types of sources are able to produce cool and bright light and are a substitute for convention lighting devices such as incandescent lamps. The special features of LED-based lighting are high luminescent efficiency, long life, environment-friendly, power saving, and smaller in size [30,31]. White light is generated by two different combinations:
1.
blue+yellow+green phosphor coupled with near-UV (NUV) LED chip and
2.
blue+yellow phosphor coupled with NUV LED chip.

Amongst these, the first combination of phosphor is useful for the fabrication of white LEDs, but it requires an excitation wavelength in the range of the NUV (350–420nm) spectral region [18,19,37]. But the light produced by the second combination does not meet the required quality of white light due to poor CRI. Currently, many researchers are busy in developing novel phosphor materials that have good CRI, better thermal stability, and good QEs, which may potentially be applicable in the development of high-brightness LEDs.

The rare earth (Eu2+, Ce3+,Tb3+, Sm3+, Dy3+, etc.) dopant with different stable host materials such as silicates, aluminates, borates, aluminosilicates, etc. shows excellent luminescence properties which correspond to the UV-to-visible region of the spectrum. Among the different lanthanide elements used in display devices, some of the rare earth ions show broadband emission under the near-UV excitation wavelength due to d-f transition and some of them show sharp band due to f-f transition. Therefore, such highly efficient sharp- and broadband emission in rare earth ions is used to fabricate LED display panel, plasma display panel, etc.

Material properties have an immense effect on the luminescence performance of nanophosphors. The thermodynamics and chemical kinetics involved during the synthesis process remarkably affect the composition, atomic structure, and defect formation that leads to varying luminescence properties. Conventionally, nanophosphors can be easily prepared by using methods such as sol–gel method [41,42], coprecipitation method [43,44], sonochemical method [45,46], microwave-assisted method [47,48], combustion method [49,50], solvothermal method [51,52], etc.

The sol-gel method is one of the most reliable methods that assure uniform particle size distribution. This method is often implemented to produce phosphors with nanosized or micron-sized particles. It also allows good control over the morphology of the nanophosphors. Precursors in the form of nitrates are generally preferred for this type of synthesis. The precursors along with a suitable surfactant and chelating agent are dissolved in deionized distilled water or some other suitable solvent. This solution is continuously stirred along with heating at moderately high temperature. The slow evaporation of the solvent will result in the formation of a sol, which will later get converted into gel. Heat treatment of this gel is necessary to eradicate the organic content present in them.

Coprecipitation methods are mostly preferred for synthesizing fluoride-, oxide-, or oxyfluoride-based nanophosphors. Mostly, precursors are in the form of chloride, nitrate, or acetate. A solvent such as distilled water, ethanol, cyclohexane, or N,N-dimethyl formaldehyde dissolves the precursors to form a solution. With constant stirring, a precipitating agent is slowly added dropwise, and this causes the precipitation of the desired compound. The precipitate is washed several times with distilled water to remove any water-soluble impurities. The precipitate is then dried in a hot air oven.

The use of high-intensity ultrasound started gaining attention for the synthesis of nanophosphors at the time when other synthesis methods produced complications such as attaining higher pressure and temperatures along with longer duration of time. The synthesis method involving high-intensity ultrasound came to be known as sonochemical synthesis. In this method, an acoustic cavitation process takes place that leads to the formation, growth, and impulsive collapse of bubbles [45]. The production of nanophosphors at room temperatures can be facilitated by the physical and chemical effects of ultrasound without going through any high pressure/temperature treatments. This method can be considered as an extended version of the coprecipitation method that uses ultrasonic waves as an additional tool. Just by monitoring the precursor compositions and the reaction conditions, a wide variety of morphologies can be achieved.

The microwave-assisted synthesis method has emerged a highly effective technique for the production of nanomaterials. Microwaves have the ability to produce very high temperatures at ambient conditions, and this fact brings it a step ahead of conventional heating. It requires very less energy and shorter duration of heating to produce nanoparticles with high purity. Microwaves induce heat by targeting the molecule with dipole nature to stimulate a chemical reaction between the precursors. Hence, it is highly recommended to dissolve the precursors in a polar solvent.

The combustion method is one of the easiest methods employed for the synthesis of nanophosphors. It is generally proceeds in two different ways. One is the simple combustion method, which is also termed “modified solid state reaction.” The other one is the solution combustion method that involves a wet-chemical synthesis process. The solution combustion method resembles the sol–gel method to some extent and is time consuming, whereas the simple combustion method is a rapidly occurring method that involves the pulverizing of precursors in a mortar–pestle and heating them at high temperatures close to 550°C. In both the cases, the reaction requires the presence of both oxidizers and fuel. The precursors in the form of nitrates form the oxidizers while chemicals such as urea, glycine, and carbohydrazide act as fuels. The redox reaction between the oxidizer and the fuel initiates a highly exothermic condition with temperature close to 1200°C that rapidly produces chemically homogenous compounds with fewer impurities than the solid-state reaction method. The process is accompanied by a flame that lasts no more than 13 seconds. This method faces a demerit that most of the times the final product is polycrystalline or amorphous in nature. In such cases, it is necessary to anneal the samples at 600–800°C. But the duration of the annealing should be kept short to avoid any merger of the particles.

In the solvothermal method, a chemical reaction among the precursors dissolved in a solvent is induced in a closed system with the aid of high temperatures and pressures. This method can be used for both aqueous and nonaqueous solvents. When the solvent used in water, this method is called the hydrothermal method. The precursors are dissolved in the solvent and sealed in an autoclave that acts as a closed system. On heating the autoclave at temperatures slightly above the boiling point of the solvent, the pressure and temperature of the solution elevates and the dissolved material crystallizes. It is possible to obtain homogenous nanoparticles of phosphors by adjusting the reaction conditions, pH, solvent, and precursor composition.

Nepheline-structured NaAlSiO4:Dy3+ phosphors were synthesized by Kumar et al. by employing the sol–gel combustion synthesis method [53]. The prepared phosphors were calcined at different temperatures ranging from 800 to 1200°C for 3h each. The band gap values were found to decrease from 4.42 to 4.32eV as the calcination temperature was increased from 600 to 1200°C. The transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and selected-area electron diffraction pattern images of the prepared samples clearly show that the nanophosphor particles had average dimensions of 48–56nm (Fig. 13.1 ). The photoluminescence (PL) emission spectrum, obtained for 348nm excitation, shows two characteristic emission peaks at 485 and 574nm. The peak at 485nm in the blue region corresponds to the magnetic dipole 4F9/2 → 6H15/2 transition, whereas the peak at 574nm in the yellow region corresponds to the electric dipole 4F9/2 → 6H13/2 transition [54]. Although, the electric dipole transition is a forbidden transition, it becomes partially allowed if the dopant (Dy3+ in this case) occupies a noncentrosymmetric site in the host lattice. The emission spectrum showed maximum intensity when the Dy3+ concentration was 1.5mol%. Depending on the concentration of Dy3+, the yellow-to-blue intensity ratio is found to be adjusted and the color output is tuned to color coordinates closer to white light.

A novel color-tunable Ba5Zn4Y8O21:Eu3+ nanophosphor has been synthesized by the urea-assisted solution combustion method by Dalal et al. [55]. The prepared phosphor crystallized in a pure tetragonal phase and appeared with semi-spherical, ellipse-shaped particle morphology. From the TEM results, the particle size is found to range from 60 to 90nm, and this was confirmed theoretically using Scherrer's equation. The first principle calculations were performed using the CASTEP (CAmbridge Serial Total Energy Package) code, and it revealed the density of states and the electronic band structure of Ba5Zn4Y8O21 host. The band gap (Eg =2.872eV) value as determined from the calculations were found suitable for Eu3+ doping to produce luminescence. A versatile luminescence emission was observed for Eu3+ ions doped in Ba5Zn4Y8O21 host.

Normally, it is routine to find the luminescence emission at 596 and 614nm with a sharp narrow band. Nevertheless, Ba5Zn4Y8O21 host produced Eu3+ luminescence in almost the entire range of the visible light spectrum. Several sharp peaks were observed between 400 and 650nm, when the phosphor was excited at 395nm. A similar emission profile was observed when the excitation wavelength was set at 274nm, as shown in Fig. 13.2 . Furthermore, varying the concentration of Eu3+ doping made it possible to tune the color emission from blue to white to red. It was observed that the emission peaks in the blue and red regions were dominant for lower concentrations of Eu3+, and as the dopant concentration increased, the emission peaks in the red region started to rise and dominate, whereas those in the blue and green region started diminishing gradually. Consequently, it became conceivable to develop color-tunable nanophosphors that could adjust the emission color from blue to white and then to red by varying the Eu3+ concentration.

Lin et al. synthesized boron nitride (BN)-coated Ca1−xSrxS:Eu2+ nanowires by a solid–liquid–solid process. The details of the synthesis have been reported in their research article [56]. The CaSrS:Eu nanowires are protected by the BN sheaths, which enhances the chemical stability of nanowires. These nanowires showed a broadband red emission with the peak centered at 645nm during high-spatial cathodoluminescence (CL) measurements. The red-color emission was tuned effectively by tailoring the composition of the nanophosphor. By increasing the Sr content in this nanowire, a blueshift is observed in the CL emission spectrum. The orange emission has been enhanced by introducing the Ce3+ codopant in CaS:Eu2+ nanowires. The presence of Ce3+ has also enhanced the luminescence intensity of Eu2+ emission in CaS nanowires, and this can be attributed to the effectively occurring energy transfer process from Ce3+ to Eu2+ ions.

Bedyal et al. synthesized an orange-red–emitting NaCaBO3:Sm3+ nanophosphor by the combustion method [49]. The particle sizes were found to be in the range of 35–45nm, and were evidenced from the TEM images. The PL excitation spectra show a broad and intense charge-transfer (CT) band from 200 to 240nm. There are some sharp excitation peaks in the range 340–420nm, and the peak at 403nm is found to be prominent among them all. The emission spectrum shows the characteristic emission lines of Sm3+ ion at 567, 600, 647, and 710nm corresponding to the transition from the excited state 4G5/2 to the 6H5/2, 6H7/2, 6H9/2, and 6H11/2, respectively. The emission spectra were analyzed for a series of samples doped with different concentrations of Sm3+ ions. It was observed that the emission intensity increased until the Sm3+ concentration was 2mol%, and beyond this limit, the intensity decreased owing to the concentration quenching phenomenon. The CL spectra were recorded by keeping the emission current constant at 1mA, and the electron beam excitation was altered from 2 to 4kV. The CL emission spectra exhibited characteristic peaks of Sm3+ at 569, 602, 650 and 710nm. The CIE 1931 chromaticity diagram shows orange emission of phosphor, and the coordinates were found to be (0.6, 0.4) and (0.57, 0.41) for the PL and CL spectra, respectively. This nanophosphor is a potential candidate for orange-red–emitting phosphors for LEDs.

Mhlongo et al. successfully incorporated the green-emitting ZnO nanoparticles into SiO2:Pr3+ by the sol–gel method and developed ZnO·SiO2:Pr3+ nanocomposite phosphor [57]. The ZnO nanocrystals were initially synthesized by the chemical precipitation method. Thereafter, SiO2:Pr3+ nanophosphors were synthesized by the sol–gel method. The synthesized nanophosphors were divided into two parts; one was incorporated with ZnO nanoparticles and the other was not. In both the cases, the red emission from the 3P0- and 1D2-emitting levels of Pr3+ was observed. However, the sample incorporated with ZnO enhanced the red emission of Pr3+ ions almost twice the intensity of the sample without ZnO incorporation. Here, there is no overlap between excitation of Pr3+ and emission of ZnO. Still, the energy transfer is assumed to take place by a photon-mediated process, and this has resulted in the enhancement of Pr3+ emission for the ZnO-embedded sample. A schematic view of the energy transfer mechanism can be witnessed from Fig. 13.3 .

ZrW2O8:Eu3+ nanophosphors were synthesized by hydrothermal route in acid/alcohol mixtures under mild conditions by Liao et al. [58]. The as-prepared nanophosphors exhibited aggregated nanorods of 60nm in diameter. The scanning electron microscope and TEM images along with the energy-dispersive spectroscopy spectra of ZrW2O8:Eu3+ nanophosphors are shown in Fig. 13.4 . The photoluminescence spectra revealed that this nanophosphor could be efficiently excited with 466-nm blue light, and under this excitation, the nanophosphor gave red emissions with CIE color coordinates (0.638, 0.361). One of the peculiar feature observed in the excitation spectrum of this nanophosphor is that the peak at 466nm is highly intense than the normally observed CT band. As shown in Fig. 13.5 , the optimum PL intensity is observed for 9mol% concentration of Eu3+. ZrW2O8:Eu3+ nanophosphors show a quantum efficiency of 83.5%, which may be less than the commercially available Y2O3:Eu phosphors. But it is higher than several other red-emitting phosphors and this makes it a potential candidate for red component in wLEDs.

Lu3Al5O12:Ce3+ garnet phosphors, more commonly known as LuAG:Ce, are well known for their excellent scintillation properties [59,60]. Apart from this, they are also known to produce excellent green luminescence emission [61,62]. Generally, Ce3+ ions produce UV emission when they are doped in some host material. But in garnets, they produce luminescence emission in the yellow–green region owing to large crystal field splitting. LuAG:Ce nanogarnets produce emission peaks with broad nature that are cantered at 505nm under 440nm excitation [63]. Praveena et al. have evaluated the thermal stability of this nanogarnet by correlating the dependence of PL intensity on the temperature at which it is recorded [63]. It is highly essential that phosphors show low thermal quenching to maintain the color chromaticity and light output. LuAG:Ce nanogarnets are found to show excellent thermal stability, even better than that of commercial yellow-emitting YAG:Ce. Fig. 13.6A shows the temperature-dependent PL emission spectra of LuAG:Ce nanogarnet which is excited at 460nm. Fig. 13.6B shows the plot of PL intensity versus temperature for the LuAG:Ce and YAG:Ce nanogarnets. The PL intensity remains nearly the same from 25 to 150°C for LuAG:Ce, whereas YAG:Ce shows gradual decrease in PL intensity with increasing temperature.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Color-tunable X3MgSi2O8:Eu2+, Mn2+ (X=Ba, Sr, Ca) Nanophosphors
ce:section.ce:para: Kim et al. synthesized X3MgSi2O8:Eu2+, Mn2+ (X=Ba, Sr, Ca) nanophosphors by employing the combustion method [64]. The mean particle size of the nanophosphor was found to be 200nm. The dopant Eu2+ ion can occupy three different sites in this family of nanophosphors. All these sites belong to the divalent X2+ ion, where X=Ba, Sr, Ca. The Eu2+ ions occupying the first X2+ site gave blue emission, whereas in the second and third X2+ sites, it gave green emission. In addition, the red emission by the Mn2+ ions occupying the Mg2+ sites contributed to the blending of red–green–blue colors for producing white light. The PL emission spectra of X3MgSi2O8:Eu2+, Mn2+ (X=Ba, Sr, Ca) nanophosphors can be seen from Fig. 13.7 . An added advantage is that these nanophosphors show maximum PL excitation intensity at 375nm, and hence, they may be efficiently excited using InGaN chips.

Kim et al. synthesized M2MgSi2O7:Eu2+ (M=Sr, Ba) nanophosphors utilizing the urea-assisted combustion method [65]. Sr2MgSi2O7:Eu2+ exhibited an intense broadband excitation ranging from 250 to 450nm with the peak centered at 355nm. The emission spectrum for 355nm excitation shows a peak at 460nm, which is purely due to the allowed 5d-4f transition Eu2+ ions. The variation of Eu doping does not have a significant effect on the emission spectra, except for the variation in intensity. On the other hand, Ba2MgSi2O7:Eu2+ shows emission spectra with two different peak centers. One peak is located at 460nm, whereas the other is at 550nm. The excitation wavelength is the same as that in case of Sr2MgSi2O7:Eu2+. On increasing the Eu-doping concentration, the peak at 460nm slightly differs and the peak at 550nm starts increasing. The emission spectra of Ba2MgSi2O7:Eu2+ nanophosphors are shown in Fig. 13.8 .

Lu et al. tailored the morphology of Ba3Si5O13:Eu2+ nanophosphor by varying the molar ratios of Ba/Si in the solution prepared during hydrothermal synthesis [66]. Ba(NO3)3 and tetraethy orthosilicate were used as the sources for Ba and Si, respectively. The shape and size of the nanophosphor altered with varying Ba:Si ratio. A hedgehog-like shape with 50–100nm particle size was observed for the ratio Ba:Si≈2:3, solid spherical particles for Ba:Si≈3:3, irregular spherical particles with small holes for Ba:Si≈6:3, worm-like structures for Ba:Si≈6:6, and worm shell-like structures for Ba:Si≈6:10. The field-emission scanning electron microscopy images of the Ba3Si5O13:Eu2+ nanophosphors for different Ba:Si ratios is shown in Fig. 13.9 . The photoluminescence emission spectra of these nanophosphors cover a broad region extending from 400 to 625nm, with the peak centered at 440nm. The spectra exhibit an asymmetric nature, thus giving a possibility of two or more emission peaks being present in these spectra. This is evident from the fact that there are three types of Ba sites in the Ba3Si5O13 structure, and Eu2+ ions can occupy any or all of these Ba sites to form emission centers. The excitation spectra were obtained by monitoring the emission wavelength at 440nm. The excitation spectra extend from 300 to 400nm with two distinct peaks at 310 and 360nm. The excitation and emission spectra for the different morphologies of Ba3Si5O13:Eu2+ nanophosphors are shown in Fig. 13.10 . The results obtained for this nanophosphor indicate that this is a potential candidate for wLEDs.

Mani et al. synthesized Tb2(MoO4)3 nanophosphor by employing the sol–gel method [67]. Generally, Tb3+ ions are introduced as the dopant in some host material to produce green light emission. But here, terbium is a part of the host lattice itself. From the diffuse reflectance spectrum, the energy band gap of this nanophosphor is found to be 3.4eV. The TEM and high-resolution TEM images confirm that the as-prepared samples showed high crystallinity with spherical particles having 30–35nm size. The photoluminescence excitation spectrum for the nanophosphor shows a highly intense peak at 485nm and comparatively intense peak at 376nm. This means that the nanophosphor can be efficiently excited by a NUV-LED or a blue-LED chip. The emission spectra, obtained by exciting phosphor at 376 and 485nm, are shown in Fig. 13.11 . The emission spectra depict a similar nature in case of both the excitations. The emission peaks can be observed at 490, 545, 600, and 619nm. However, the peak at 545nm is extraordinarily intense than the other peaks. The CIE chromaticity color coordinates are located at (0.31, 0.55), which means that the overall color emission of this nanophosphor is in the green region. Tb2(MoO4)3 nanophosphor is, thus, a stable, efficient green-emitting phosphor for wLEDs.

This chapter presents the advantages of nanophosphor in the development of light-emitting diodes. It covers the brief knowledge about the significance and advantages of rare earth–doped phosphor for wLEDs and display applications, and also some existing techniques for the synthesis of phosphor materials are given in detail. The different example of rare earth–doped phosphor materials such as silicates, oxides, aluminates, tungstates, and their observed luminescence properties are discussed well. Such phosphors play the important role in the production of white light for LEDs application.
